easy: A bug is an error in a program. Finding and fixing bugs is called debugging.
Git is a version control system. It tracks changes to files over time.
An API lets two programs talk to each other. It defines a set of rules.
A server listens for requests. It sends back a response to the client.
A function takes input and returns output. It should do one thing well.
A variable stores a value in memory. It has a name and a type.
A loop repeats a block of code. It runs until a condition is false.
A database stores data in tables. Each table has rows and columns.
A test checks if code works correctly. It helps prevent future bugs.
A class defines a blueprint for objects. It groups data and behavior.
The terminal is a text-based interface. It lets you run commands directly.
An array is an ordered list of items. Each item has an index number.
A string is a sequence of characters. It is often used for text data.
A boolean can be true or false. It is used in conditional logic.
A compiler converts code to machine language. It checks for syntax errors.
DNS maps domain names to IP addresses. It works like a phone book.
HTTPS encrypts data sent over the web. It keeps user data safe.
JSON is a data format using key-value pairs. It is easy to read.
A container packages an app with its dependencies. It runs anywhere.
A pull request asks others to review your code. It starts a discussion.
A branch lets you work on features separately. You merge it when done.
CSS styles the look of a web page. It controls colors, fonts, and layout.
HTML defines the structure of a page. It uses tags to mark content.
A package manager installs libraries for you. It handles dependencies.
Logging records events in your application. It helps with debugging.
A token is used for authentication. It proves who the user is.
Caching stores data for quick access later. It reduces load on servers.
A webhook sends data when an event happens. It triggers an action.
A proxy sits between a client and a server. It forwards requests.
A linter checks code for style issues. It helps keep code consistent.
medium: Microservices split a large application into small, independent services. Each service handles one business function and communicates over the network. This makes scaling and deploying easier.
A CI/CD pipeline automates building, testing, and deploying code. When you push changes to a repository, the pipeline runs automatically. This reduces human error and speeds up delivery.
Docker containers package applications with all their dependencies. Unlike virtual machines, containers share the host operating system kernel. This makes them lightweight and fast to start.
TypeScript adds static types to JavaScript. It catches many bugs at compile time rather than runtime. This improves code quality and developer productivity.
React uses a virtual DOM to efficiently update the user interface. When state changes, React calculates the minimal set of DOM updates needed. This approach improves rendering performance.
A REST API uses HTTP methods like GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE. Resources are identified by URLs. Responses typically use JSON format for data exchange.
GraphQL lets clients request exactly the data they need. Unlike REST, a single endpoint handles all queries. This prevents over-fetching and under-fetching of data.
WebSockets enable real-time, bidirectional communication between client and server. Unlike HTTP, the connection stays open after the initial handshake. This is ideal for chat apps and live updates.
OAuth allows users to grant limited access to their accounts. It uses tokens instead of sharing passwords. Many services use OAuth for third-party authentication.
Kubernetes orchestrates container deployment across multiple machines. It handles scaling, load balancing, and self-healing automatically. This is essential for running production workloads reliably.
A load balancer distributes incoming traffic across multiple servers. It prevents any single server from being overwhelmed. This improves both reliability and response times.
SQL databases use structured tables with defined schemas. They enforce data integrity through constraints and relationships. PostgreSQL and MySQL are popular open source options.
NoSQL databases store data in flexible, schema-less formats. They excel at handling large volumes of unstructured data. MongoDB and Redis are widely used examples.
Unit tests verify that individual functions work correctly in isolation. They run fast and catch regressions early. Writing tests before code is called test-driven development.
Agile development breaks work into short iterations called sprints. Teams deliver working software incrementally. Daily standups keep everyone aligned on progress and blockers.
A message queue decouples producers from consumers. Messages are stored until the consumer is ready to process them. This improves system reliability and handles traffic spikes.
Serverless computing lets you run code without managing servers. The cloud provider handles infrastructure and scaling. You only pay for the compute time you actually use.
An ORM maps database tables to objects in your programming language. It lets you query data without writing raw SQL. Prisma and Sequelize are popular choices for Node.js.
Web accessibility ensures websites work for people with disabilities. Screen readers need semantic HTML and ARIA labels. Good accessibility also improves the experience for all users.
A CDN caches content on servers around the world. Users download files from the nearest server location. This reduces latency and improves page load times significantly.
Environment variables store configuration outside your source code. They let you change settings without redeploying. Secrets like API keys should never be committed to version control.
Rate limiting controls how many requests a client can make. It prevents abuse and protects server resources. Most APIs return a 429 status code when the limit is exceeded.
A design pattern is a reusable solution to a common problem. The observer pattern notifies subscribers about state changes. The factory pattern creates objects without specifying exact classes.
Continuous integration means merging code changes frequently. Each merge triggers automated tests to catch issues early. This practice reduces integration problems and keeps the codebase healthy.
A reverse proxy receives requests and forwards them to backend servers. It can handle SSL termination and caching. Nginx and Caddy are commonly used as reverse proxies.
Functional programming treats computation as the evaluation of pure functions. It avoids mutable state and side effects. This makes code easier to test and reason about.
A monorepo stores multiple projects in a single repository. It simplifies dependency management and code sharing. Tools like Turborepo and Nx help manage large monorepos efficiently.
Event-driven architecture uses events to trigger and communicate between services. An event bus routes messages to interested listeners. This creates loosely coupled and highly scalable systems.
Code review is the practice of having others examine your changes. It catches bugs, shares knowledge, and improves code quality. Most teams require at least one approval before merging.

Infrastructure as code defines servers and networks using configuration files. Tools like Terraform apply these definitions automatically. This makes infrastructure reproducible and version-controlled.
hard: Distributed systems face unique challenges around consistency, availability, and partition tolerance. The CAP theorem states that you can only guarantee two of these three properties at once. Understanding these trade-offs is critical when designing systems that span multiple data centers. Engineers must carefully choose between strong consistency and eventual consistency based on their application requirements.

Concurrency bugs are notoriously difficult to reproduce and fix because they depend on timing. Race conditions occur when multiple threads access shared data without proper synchronization. Deadlocks happen when two or more threads wait for each other to release locks. Using immutable data structures and message passing can help avoid many of these issues in practice.

Database indexing dramatically improves query performance by creating optimized data structures. A B-tree index allows logarithmic time lookups, insertions, and deletions. However, each index adds overhead to write operations and consumes additional storage. Choosing the right indexes requires analyzing query patterns and balancing read performance against write costs.

The event loop is the core mechanism that enables asynchronous programming in JavaScript. It continuously checks the call stack and processes messages from the task queue. Microtasks from promises are given higher priority than macrotasks like setTimeout callbacks. Understanding this execution model is essential for writing performant and predictable JavaScript applications.

Garbage collection automatically reclaims memory that is no longer being used by a program. Modern collectors use generational algorithms that separate objects by their age. Young objects are collected frequently while older objects are scanned less often. Tuning garbage collection parameters can significantly impact application latency and throughput in production systems.

Cross-site scripting attacks inject malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users. Stored XSS persists on the server, while reflected XSS comes through request parameters. Content Security Policy headers restrict which scripts can execute on a page. Input sanitization and output encoding are essential defenses that every web developer should implement.

Observability combines logging, metrics, and distributed tracing to understand system behavior. Structured logs with correlation IDs allow engineers to trace requests across multiple services. Metrics provide aggregated views of system health over time. Together, these tools enable teams to detect, diagnose, and resolve production issues quickly and efficiently.

Blue-green deployments maintain two identical production environments to minimize downtime during releases. Traffic is routed to the blue environment while green receives the new version. After testing, traffic switches to green instantly. If issues are found, rolling back is as simple as switching traffic back to the blue environment.

Content addressable storage uses the hash of data as its identifier rather than a file path. Git uses this approach to store every version of every file efficiently. If two files have identical content, only one copy is stored. This design enables powerful features like deduplication, integrity verification, and efficient branching and merging operations.

The principle of least privilege states that every component should have only the minimum permissions needed. In Kubernetes, this means using RBAC to restrict service account access to specific resources. Network policies limit which pods can communicate with each other. Applying this principle consistently reduces the blast radius of security breaches significantly.

Feature flags allow teams to deploy code to production without exposing new features to all users. They enable gradual rollouts, A/B testing, and instant kill switches for problematic features. Server-side flags provide more control while client-side flags reduce latency. A well-designed flag system should include cleanup workflows to remove stale flags regularly.

Functional reactive programming combines functional programming with reactive data streams. Observables emit values over time, and operators like map, filter, and merge transform these streams. This paradigm excels at handling complex asynchronous workflows such as user interactions and real-time data feeds. Libraries like RxJS bring these concepts to everyday JavaScript development.

Database sharding horizontally partitions data across multiple database instances to handle massive scale. A shard key determines which instance stores each record. Range-based sharding distributes data by value ranges while hash-based sharding ensures more even distribution. Resharding existing data is one of the most complex operations in distributed database management.

Zero-downtime migrations require careful planning when changing database schemas in production systems. The expand-contract pattern first adds new columns or tables alongside existing ones. Application code is updated to write to both old and new schemas simultaneously. Only after all data has been migrated and verified can the old schema be safely removed.

Container orchestration platforms like Kubernetes use declarative configuration to manage application deployments. A deployment manifest specifies the desired state including replica count and resource limits. The control plane continuously reconciles actual state with desired state. This self-healing behavior ensures applications remain available even when individual nodes fail unexpectedly.

WebAssembly compiles languages like Rust, C, and Go to a portable binary format that runs in browsers. It executes at near-native speed, making it suitable for performance-critical applications. The module system allows WebAssembly to interact with JavaScript seamlessly. Use cases range from image processing and gaming to running entire applications like Figma in the browser.

Eventual consistency is a model where replicas converge to the same state given enough time without new updates. Conflict-free replicated data types provide mathematical guarantees about convergence without coordination. CRDTs enable collaborative editing in applications like Google Docs and Figma. Understanding the trade-offs between consistency models is essential for distributed system design.

The actor model organizes computation around independent actors that communicate through message passing. Each actor has private state and processes messages sequentially from its mailbox. This eliminates shared mutable state and makes concurrency safer by design. Erlang pioneered this approach, and modern systems like Akka bring it to the JVM ecosystem.

Service mesh architecture adds a proxy sidecar to every service instance in your infrastructure. These proxies handle cross-cutting concerns like load balancing, encryption, and observability. Istio and Linkerd are popular implementations that work with Kubernetes deployments. This approach separates networking logic from application code, reducing complexity for individual service teams.

Chaos engineering intentionally injects failures into production systems to build confidence in resilience. Netflix pioneered this practice with Chaos Monkey, which randomly terminates virtual machine instances. More sophisticated experiments test network partitions, dependency failures, and resource exhaustion. The key principle is to start with a hypothesis about system behavior and verify it through controlled experiments.

Write-ahead logging ensures database durability by recording changes to a log before applying them. If the system crashes during a write operation, the log can replay uncommitted changes during recovery. PostgreSQL uses WAL for crash recovery and also for streaming replication to standby servers. Understanding WAL mechanics is essential for database administrators tuning performance and reliability.

Memory-safe languages like Rust prevent entire categories of bugs at compile time without garbage collection. The ownership system ensures that each value has exactly one owner, and borrowing rules prevent data races. Lifetimes track how long references are valid, eliminating use-after-free vulnerabilities. These guarantees make Rust ideal for systems programming where both safety and performance are critical.

API versioning strategies help maintain backward compatibility as interfaces evolve over time. URL path versioning is the simplest approach but creates multiple endpoints to maintain. Header-based versioning keeps URLs clean but is harder to test in browsers. Content negotiation uses Accept headers to let clients specify their preferred version of the response format.

Consensus algorithms enable distributed systems to agree on shared state despite node failures. Raft breaks consensus into leader election, log replication, and safety components for clarity. Paxos is theoretically optimal but notoriously difficult to implement correctly in practice. These algorithms are the foundation of distributed databases, configuration stores, and coordination services.

Progressive web applications combine the reach of web pages with the capabilities of native apps. Service workers enable offline functionality by intercepting network requests and serving cached responses. The web app manifest allows installation on home screens with custom icons and splash screens. Push notifications keep users engaged even when the browser is closed.

Compiler optimization transforms source code into efficient machine instructions through multiple passes. Dead code elimination removes unreachable statements, while constant folding evaluates expressions at compile time. Loop unrolling trades code size for reduced branch overhead in tight loops. Understanding these optimizations helps developers write code that compilers can optimize most effectively.

Immutable infrastructure treats servers as disposable resources that are replaced rather than updated in place. New versions are built from scratch using automated pipelines and tested before deployment. This approach eliminates configuration drift and ensures every environment is reproducible. Combined with infrastructure as code, it provides a reliable foundation for continuous delivery practices.

Time-series databases are optimized for storing and querying data points indexed by timestamp. They use compression algorithms tailored to sequential numeric data, achieving high storage efficiency. InfluxDB and TimescaleDB are popular choices for metrics, IoT sensor data, and financial records. Downsampling and retention policies help manage the enormous volume of data these systems typically ingest.

The twelve-factor app methodology defines best practices for building modern, scalable web applications. It emphasizes strict separation between configuration and code, treating backing services as attached resources. Logs should be treated as event streams, and admin processes should run as one-off tasks. Following these principles makes applications portable across different cloud platforms and easy to scale horizontally.

Edge computing moves processing closer to where data is generated rather than sending everything to central servers. This reduces latency for real-time applications like autonomous vehicles and augmented reality. Edge nodes can preprocess and filter data before forwarding relevant information to the cloud. Challenges include managing distributed state, handling intermittent connectivity, and ensuring security across many edge locations.
